text: Everyone forgets Jokic averaged a triple-double through the playoffs while basically carrying a roster with no other All-Star. You don't fluke that. Volume and efficiency, from a center, and we've genuinely never seen it before.
label: analysis

text: The on/off splits say it all. Wolves were +9.1 per 100 with Gobert anchoring the paint and roughly league average without him. Rim protection travels in the playoffs, that's the whole reason they jumped a tier.
label: analysis

text: Comparing him to MJ kinda ignores pace. Jordan played in an era around 91 possessions a game, we're closer to 99 now. Per possession the scoring gap is way smaller than the raw PPG makes it look.
label: analysis

text: Boston's whole thing is the 0.5 second rule, catch decide move within half a second. It's why their corner three rate led the league and their assist percentage jumped after the Porzingis trade.
label: analysis

text: The Clippers gave up a 27 year old All-NBA wing, an All-Star guard and a pile of unprotected picks for George. Like the player or not, that pick capital is what kept them out of the lottery protection conversation for years.
label: analysis

text: Embiid's free throw rate is basically his whole offense. When the refs let teams get physical in the playoffs his TS% drops like six points, and that's the difference between a 30 piece and a 22 point grind.
label: analysis

text: Historically, teams that win the offensive rebounding battle by 5+ in a series win it around 70% of the time. OKC crashing the glass isn't random, it's a deliberate variance play.
label: analysis

text: Chart his shot diet and 38% of his attempts are pull up threes. Those are hard shots, so the low efficiency takes kinda ignore how tough the looks he's creating actually are.
label: analysis

text: Pacers offense fell off for a simple spacing reason. With Turner stretching the floor Haliburton had a driving lane. No real five who shoots and the paint clogs, then his assist numbers crater.
label: analysis

text: Furthest draft pick to make an All-Star team is pretty much Isaiah Thomas at 60th before the injuries got him. Sikma went 8th, so the 'nobody saw it coming' framing doesn't really hold up.
label: analysis

text: Win shares get noisy in small samples but across 82 games they line up well with team success. His 13.2 last year was top five in the league and the eye test backs up the volume.
label: analysis

text: Switch everything only works if all five guys can actually hold up on an island. The second you've got one non-switchable defender, playoff offenses hunt him every possession.
label: analysis

text: The Luka trade looks lopsided because Dallas took back an injury prone center and a role player for a 25 year old top three offensive engine. Debate the fit all you want, on pure asset value it's hard to defend.
label: analysis

text: His true shooting jumped from 56 to 61 after they staggered his minutes with the bench unit. More touches against second units pumped up the efficiency, so context matters before you crown the leap as real growth.
label: analysis

text: Defensive rating doesn't capture how much he disrupts without fouling. His deflection rate and the fact opponents shot 9% worse at the rim against him tell a story the box score totally misses.
label: analysis

text: The 73 win Warriors had a +10.8 net rating. This year's best team is at +8.9. The regular season dominance just isn't on the same level historically, even if the talent feels comparable.
label: analysis

text: Rookie scoring is up across the league mostly because of pace and three point volume. Adjust for usage and the efficiency for this class is pretty pedestrian.
label: analysis

text: Factor in that he played 30% of his minutes at the five in small ball lineups and the rebounding is actually below average for that role. Position matters when you're throwing around per game boards.
label: analysis

text: That comeback worked because of a zone wrinkle. They went 2-3 for six possessions, forced three turnovers, and the whole run was built on those live ball giveaways, not some sudden shooting surge.
label: analysis

text: People give the coach credit for the turnaround but the schedule got way easier. They played the softest stretch in the league by opponent win percentage during that 12-2 run, so regress the hype accordingly.
label: analysis

text: His 3.1 assist to turnover ratio is elite for a high usage guard. Stack him up against other primary creators carrying 30%+ usage and you see how rare that kind of ball security really is.
label: analysis

text: Clutch stats are small sample but over three seasons he's shooting 47% on clutch shots on real volume. That's not a hot streak anymore, that's just a skill at this point.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs development model has always been passing IQ over raw athleticism. It's why their second rounders keep outplaying their draft slot compared to other franchises.
label: analysis

text: Isolate his half court possessions, strip out transition, and the efficiency falls to the 40th percentile. A lot of his scoring is pace driven, which is exactly what playoff defenses are built to take away.
label: analysis

text: The real story is the cap math. They're hard capped at the first apron, so anything they take back has to fit within about $8M of outgoing salary. That limits the trade tree more than fans realize.
label: analysis

text: Three point variance decides modern playoff series more than people want to admit. The team that shot better from deep won 11 of the last 14 conference finals. Shot quality matters, but so does luck on a given night.
label: analysis

text: His usage went up four points and the efficiency held, which is the hardest thing to pull off in this league. Most guys fall off a cliff when you hand them more shot creation.
label: analysis

text: Across eras, defensive three seconds and the hand check rules completely changed perimeter scoring. You can't just compare a 30 PPG season in 2005 to one now without adjusting for the rule changes.
label: analysis

text: Their bench struggled and the lineup data shows why. Most used second unit had a -7 net rating while the starters were +12. Stagger the stars more and that gap closes.
label: analysis

text: He shot 40% from three catch and shoot but only 31% off the dribble. Defenses know it, so they go under every screen and dare him to pull up, which is why his scoring stalled in the half court.
label: analysis

text: Rebounding is partly a team stat. His individual numbers look great but the team grabbed a lower share of available boards with him on the floor, which says he's vacuuming up teammates' rebounds.
label: analysis

text: The Paul George return for Indiana years back netted Sabonis and Oladipo, and Oladipo made All-NBA the next year. Context wise that's still one of the better PG returns we've seen.
label: analysis

text: His plus minus is propped up by playing next to two All-Stars. Look at his minutes without them and the lineups are barely above water, which complicates the whole MVP thing.
label: analysis

text: Pace adjusted, the defense gave up 112 per 100, good for 18th. The elite defense label comes from raw points allowed, but they play slow, so the per possession number is more honest.
label: analysis

text: The trade deadline was quiet because of the new CBA. Second apron penalties make teams hoard expirings and avoid aggregating salary, so the volume of moves dropped league wide this year.
label: analysis

text: Watch the film and the double comes a beat late every single time, which is how he's been picking teams apart. It's a coverage problem, not just him being brilliant.
label: analysis

text: Career playoff splits matter here. He shoots 4% worse from the field and his turnover rate climbs in elimination games over a decent sample. That's a real trend, not a couple bad nights.
label: analysis

text: Offensive rating with him running the show is 119 and it drops to 108 with the backup. That 11 point swing is basically the whole reason they're a playoff team and not a lottery team.
label: analysis

text: Free agency value is all about opportunity cost. Overpaying a good not great wing on a long deal is what kills your flexibility three years out, which is the exact trap they just walked into.
label: analysis

text: Eye test says he's a great defender but the tracking data says he gambles for steals and gives up backdoor cuts a ton. High event defense isn't always efficient defense.
label: analysis

text: Adjust for garbage time and his fourth quarter numbers shrink a lot. A chunk of that scoring comes in blowouts, so the closer reputation isn't really backed up by the high leverage data.
label: analysis

text: The 2004 Pistons proved you can win a title without a superstar if your defense is historically good and balanced. This roster is built on the same five out, switch everything idea.
label: analysis

text: His rim finishing dropped to 58% this year, well under his career mark. The athleticism dip after the achilles is showing up in the data, even if the highlight dunks still go viral.
label: analysis

text: Strength of schedule explains a lot of the early standings. Three of the top seeds played a soft non conference slate and the metrics already project regression once it balances out.
label: analysis

text: Zone defense is creeping back because of three point volume. If you can force teams into contested above the break threes, you're mathematically lowering their expected points per possession.
label: analysis

text: Look at the four factors, they win turnover rate and free throw rate but lose the shooting battle most nights. That's a fragile formula because turnovers are the least repeatable of the four.
label: analysis

text: His contract is actually team friendly when you line the cap hit up against comparable players. Locking a 26 year old All-Star at 28% of the cap is a steal in a rising cap environment.
label: analysis

text: Defenses are blitzing the pick and roll to get the ball out of his hands and his teammates are shooting 33% on the 4 on 3s. The scheme's fine, the supporting cast just isn't punishing it.
label: analysis

text: Separate his transition threes from his half court threes and the gap is stark. Elite in the open floor, merely average set in the half court, which is exactly what playoff slowdowns expose.
label: analysis

text: The historical comp for this kind of leap is a guard who improves his pull up jumper between years two and three. That's the swing skill, and the early shot chart says it's actually happening.
label: analysis

text: Clutch net rating is volatile but their late game offense ranks dead last in points per possession. That's a coaching and personnel issue around shot selection, not just bad shooting luck.
label: analysis

text: He fits better here because of the spacing. Surround a slasher with four shooters instead of two and you completely change the geometry of his drives, and the rim attempts per game prove it.
label: analysis

text: Big men who lean on touch over explosion tend to age better. His game was never about athleticism, so projecting him to stay effective into his mid 30s is reasonable based on similar guys.
label: analysis

text: Their defense got better because they stopped switching one through five and started dropping the center in the pick and roll. Opponent rim frequency went up but the efficiency at the rim dropped, worth the trade.
label: analysis

text: MVP usually goes to the best player on a top two seed. Voting history says availability matters too, and missing 20 games has basically been a disqualifier regardless of the per game stats.
label: analysis

text: Watch the possessions and most of his assists are simple swing passes that pick up hockey assist credit. His actual creation rate, passes that lead straight to a rim shot, is a lot more modest.
label: analysis

text: The luxury tax repeater penalty is why small market teams break up cores early. It's not always talent evaluation, the financial math forces moves that look like panic from the outside.
label: analysis

text: Their offense ranks first in the half court but last in transition, which is weird. Tells you it's a deliberate, methodical scheme that wins on execution instead of chaos and pace.
label: analysis

text: He's shooting a career high from three but it's 90 attempts. Bet on regression to his career 34%. One good month isn't enough to call it a real change in his game.
label: analysis

text: Lineup data has the closing five at +15 in 200 minutes together. That's the lineup that matters in the playoffs, and it's why I'm higher on this team than the seed says.
label: analysis

text: Fouls matter so much here because of the bonus. They've got opponents in the penalty by the seven minute mark of quarters, which pumps their free throw rate and quietly props up the offensive rating.
label: analysis

text: His defensive impact shows up in opponent shot location. Teams take 6% fewer shots at the rim with him out there, settling for midrange, which is exactly what a drop coverage anchor is supposed to force.
label: analysis

text: Comparing him to the all time greats by raw counting stats ignores that he plays nine more minutes a game than they did in their primes. Per 36 the gap is real but way narrower.
label: analysis

text: The trade made sense because of the timeline. You don't pair a 22 year old building block with a 34 year old win now vet unless you've misread your own window, and the front office finally fixed that.
label: analysis

text: Their three point defense is 'good' by percentage but they allow the third most attempts in the league. That's a luck dependent profile because opponent three point percentage is the noisiest defensive stat there is.
label: analysis

text: Isolate the minutes the stars sit and the bench gets outscored by 8 per 100. That's the single biggest fixable weakness, and it's a roster construction problem more than a coaching one.
label: analysis

text: He averages 2.1 deflections and 1.4 steals but his man shoots better than league average. High activity, low discipline defense like that gets cooked by elite half court offenses in May.
label: analysis

text: That second round pick was so valuable because of the contract control. Four years of a cost controlled rotation guy is worth more in this CBA than a mid level vet on an expiring.
label: analysis

text: His scoring efficiency by zone tells the whole story: 68% at the rim, 41% midrange, 38% from three. Genuinely balanced, modern shot diet, which is why defenses can't load up on any one spot.
label: analysis

text: People keep bringing up his playoff record against top seeds as proof he's overrated, but you've gotta adjust for the rosters. In the years he had a top 15 supporting cast his series win rate against one seeds is actually above .500.
label: analysis
